content.problemStatement:
The Petrozavodsk camp takes place in about one month. Jafar wants to participate in the camp, but guess what? His coach is Yalalovichik.

Yalalovichik is a legendary coach, famous in the history of competitive programming. However, he is only willing to send to the camp students who solve really hard problems on Timus. The deadline that Yalalovichik set before has passed and he refuses to send Jafar to the camp.

Jafar decided to make Yalalovichik happy in hopes of changing his decision, so he invented a new sequence of numbers and named them Yalalovichik numbers. Jafar is writing a research paper about their properties and wants to publish it in the Science Eagle yearly journal.

A Yalalovichik number is created in the following way:


Consider an integer NNN in decimal notation; let's call it the base of the Yalalovichik number YNY_NYN​. NNN may not contain the digit 000.
Treat NNN as a decimal string. Compute all left shifts of this string N0,N1,…,N∣N∣−1N_0, N_1, \ldots, N_{|N|-1}N0​,N1​,…,N∣N∣−1​ (∣N∣|N|∣N∣ denotes the number of digits of NNN); specifically, NkN_kNk​ denotes the string formed by moving the first kkk digits of NNN to the end in the same order.
Concatenate the strings N0,N1,…,N∣N∣−1N_0, N_1, \ldots, N_{|N|-1}N0​,N1​,…,N∣N∣−1​. The resulting string is the decimal notation of YNY_NYN​.
For example, if N=123N = 123N=123, the left shifts are 123,231,312123, 231, 312123,231,312 and thus YN=123231312Y_N = 123231312YN​=123231312.

You are given the base NNN. Calculate the value of YNY_NYN​ modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: N does not contain the digit 0
The sum of |N| over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first and only line of each test case contains a single decimal integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the value of the Yalalovichik number YN modulo 10^9+7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The left shifts of the string '123' are '123', '231', and '312'. The concatenation of these strings is '123231312'. The value of this string modulo 10^9+7 is 186.
The left shifts of the string '456' are '456', '564', and '645'. The concatenation of these strings is '456564645'. The value of this string modulo 10^9+7 is 581.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use modular arithmetic to efficiently calculate the value of YN modulo 10^9+7. We start by initializing the result to 0. Then, for each left shift of the string N, we concatenate the shifted string to the result and update the result modulo 10^9+7. This approach ensures that the result does not overflow and allows us to efficiently calculate the value of YN.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * |N|) — where T is the number of test cases and |N| is the maximum length of the string N.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(|N|) — where |N| is the maximum length of the string N.
